posts.name: Mikael B E K E L E
posts.cooked:
This somehow slipped through with little notice:

https://www.wine-searcher.com/m/2021/10/jane-anson-bordeauxs-honest-broker

https://janeanson.com/

Deserves a lot more attention…

Jane Anson will/have leave decanter and do an independent Bordeaux dedicated thing similar to what others have done in Burgundy.
posts.display_username: Mikael B E K E L E

link_counts.title: Jane Anson: Bordeaux's Honest Broker | Wine-Searcher News & Features
Home - Jane Anson | Inside Bordeaux | janeanson.com

posts.name: Alexander Smith
posts.cooked: Yup, Jane was the only reason to subscribe to Decanter, imo

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked: Thanks Mikael, that’s good to know - I had no idea. As a Decanter subscriber, it is very bad news indeed she she was increasingly the only reason I bothered. I find it strange that there is no mention of her departure on the Decanter website (unless it’s very well-hidden).

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Thanks Mikael, that’s good to know - I had no idea. As a Decanter subscriber, it is very bad news indeed she she was increasingly the only reason I bothered. I find it strange that there is no mention of her departure on the Decanter website (unless it’s very well-hidden).

I’d heard about this, but didn’t actually know who she was. Quite a bit of this going around these days (and I expect to see more soon), though I wonder how much space there is for individual subscriptions. It’s even more Balkanized than streaming services, at this point!

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked: Already subscribed. She is one of the most reliable sources for Bordeaux and was so for a few years, especially for back vintages. Already quite some verticals on her new website, the notes are great, lots of details, even with decanting advice.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
Thanks Mikael, that’s good to know - I had no idea. As a Decanter subscriber, it is very bad news indeed she she was increasingly the only reason I bothered. I find it strange that there is no mention of her departure on the Decanter website (unless it’s very well-hidden).

I’d heard about this, but didn’t actually know who she was. Quite a bit of this going around these days (and I expect to see more soon), though I wonder how much space there is for individual subscriptions. It’s even more Balkanized than streaming services, at this point!

She’s a writer I’ve been aware of for twenty years but who has only really made an impression on me fairly recently, just because as you say there are so many others. I like what she writes and find her taste often corresponds to mine. But it’s already a crowded market and I’m not sure there’s room. The website looks good but I wonder whether 110 euros isn’t a bit steep for one writer covering one region.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
She’s been promoting her new product lightly now. I’ve listened to a lot of her podcasts - which are great - and think that for those who are really focusing on the region of Bordeaux that subscription is likely nothing compared to what they could/might spend. But I’m not sure she really moves prices, so getting an early call for a wine doesn’t do much good. And for ‘en primeur’ purchasers, the Chateaux new habit of holding back supply means that one can’t really monetize any insight Jane Anson might offer her subscribers, e.g. she rates the next vintage of Canon 99-100, and one gleefully calls up their favored seller trying to buy multi cases on futures. The estate releases a small tranche, the avid prospective purchasers gets only 2 bottles allocated, and the estate keeps the bulk of production in their cellars until they can sell it for much more.

In a marketplace like the above, the value of a first look on the cru classe in barrel is much reduced (I think). However if one is subscribing to find the next Sociando Mallet there should be value. I think JM Quarin of the French Wine Review had identified that property as one interest in the 70’s although the American market was slower to notice.

I think the latter use case is a different proposition, and with platforms like WB, harder nowadays. But Anson is in Bordeaux and knows everybody, so will likely still get the early call on interesting wines.

posts.name: Mikael B E K E L E
posts.cooked:
Thanks Mikael, that’s good to know - I had no idea. As a Decanter subscriber, it is very bad news indeed she she was increasingly the only reason I bothered. I find it strange that there is no mention of her departure on the Decanter website (unless it’s very well-hidden).

I’d heard about this, but didn’t actually know who she was. Quite a bit of this going around these days (and I expect to see more soon), though I wonder how much space there is for individual subscriptions. It’s even more Balkanized than streaming services, at this point!

She’s a writer I’ve been aware of for twenty years but who has only really made an impression on me fairly recently, just because as you say there are so many others. I like what she writes and find her taste often corresponds to mine. But it’s already a crowded market and I’m not sure there’s room. The website looks good but I wonder whether 110 euros isn’t a bit steep for one writer covering one region.

Will be interesting to see how it develops, and she’s maybe the only boots on the ground in Bordeaux (?). In Burgundy there are a number of well regarded reviewers already. Hopefully it will bring more/broader insight and while it is her show, a quote from the article suggest there could be quite interesting additions for those who might have a broader interest:

“I’d like to give other specialists a voice on the site, so it’s not just me – to feature articles and reports from Bordeaux historians, scientists, economists, and other experts”

Having the journalist background should give her an edge. Have not followed her extensively but what I have read of the years have been very good/interesting.
posts.display_username: Mikael B E K E L E

Hopefully it will bring more/broader insight and while it is her show, a quote from the article suggest there could be quite interesting additions for those who might have a broader interest:

“I’d like to give other specialists a voice on the site, so it’s not just me – to feature articles and reports from Bordeaux historians, scientists, economists, and other experts”

Yes, I agree - the fact she lives in Bordeaux is a definite plus. I get the impression that she has a good network of contacts and that she keeps her ear to the ground, so it could be interesting to follow what she does. It’s hard to get a head above the mêlée for any Bordeaux critic but she has certainly got some advantages. Also I agree that she seems to be very open-minded, not at all dogmatic and apart from anything else, it’s good to have another woman in the critic business. It’s a brave move and I hope she succeeds so I have signed up to see what she does.

posts.cooked:
Thanks Mikael, that’s good to know - I had no idea. As a Decanter subscriber, it is very bad news indeed she she was increasingly the only reason I bothered. I find it strange that there is no mention of her departure on the Decanter website (unless it’s very well-hidden).

I’d heard about this, but didn’t actually know who she was. Quite a bit of this going around these days (and I expect to see more soon), though I wonder how much space there is for individual subscriptions. It’s even more Balkanized than streaming services, at this point!

She’s a writer I’ve been aware of for twenty years but who has only really made an impression on me fairly recently, just because as you say there are so many others. I like what she writes and find her taste often corresponds to mine. But it’s already a crowded market and I’m not sure there’s room. The website looks good but I wonder whether 110 euros isn’t a bit steep for one writer covering one region.

My post was a bit facetious - I looked her up after I heard the news. (I’m not posting to flaunt my ignorance!) That said, I wasn’t aware of her previously. I think the “boots on the ground” type of knowledge in Bordeaux is a bit less useful than in other regions since the big estates are fairly corporate and more transparent than, say, in Burgundy. The chateaux also do barrel tastings for the wine press, etc. Given that I don’t drink Bordeaux young, there’s just a limit to how useful this info is to me. Which is one of the reasons, I think, that critics like Gilman have focused on BDX less.

I fully appreciate that my view on this is not universal, of course.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
In a marketplace like the above, the value of a first look on the cru classe in barrel is much reduced (I think). However if one is subscribing to find the next Sociando Mallet there should be value. I think JM Quarin of the French Wine Review had identified that property as one interest in the 70’s although the American market was slower to notice.

I think the latter use case is a different proposition, and with platforms like WB, harder nowadays. But Anson is in Bordeaux and knows everybody, so will likely still get the early call on interesting wines.

Are you sure? There are still opportunities I think: Canon’s average Wine Searcher price is 150% above the En Primeur price for 2015, and 53% above for 2016, 23% for 2018 and 16% for 2020. Same can be said for many other wines in these vintages. The outstanding wines (either very high critic scores or very high vs price level) in these vintages performed well since En Primeur. But you had to do some research to identify these wines (i.e. critic scores matter).

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I think the “boots on the ground” type of knowledge in Bordeaux is a bit less useful than in other regions since the big estates are fairly corporate and more transparent than, say, in Burgundy.

I can see why you might think that, but in reality there is a lot of interesting stuff going on in the vineyards that hasn’t been all that well reported on. You are still more likely to hear the name of the architect who designed the tasting room than the name of the vineyard manager from the wine press. So there’s definitely room to improve here.

I couldn’t agree more. Obviously the last 18 months have paused most in-depth reporting, so undoubtedly that adds to the perception that there hasn’t been much! But even before, there was too much focus on the annual EP jamboree, with the plethora of tasting notes by a swarm of critics, plus the odd prestige vertical - so yes, quite a lot left to be desired.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
It has been a few weeks since I signed up to Jane Anson’s site and I must say that I’m really rather impressed.

Obviously the content is just starting to add up, but the reports and articles are now arriving thick and fast, including some fascinating insight into the plight of Crus Bourgeois - she points out that their financial situation is increasingly precarious, even the best ones, and the future does not look very rosy, which is very bad news for consumers. I don’t remember anyone else covering this.

Decanter’s loss is her gain, I think - she really is “boots on the ground”.

PS: I forgot, a Gruaud vertical - where finally someone has reviewed the 2019 - and which sounds excellent.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
Obviously the content is just starting to add up, but the reports and articles are now arriving thick and fast, including some fascinating insight into the plight of Crus Bourgeois - she points out that their financial situation is increasingly precarious, even the best ones, and the future does not look very rosy, which is very bad news for consumers.

I’ve been hearing rumblings of this for many years…is there something new/different in 2021?

It seems to me that the more pressing issue for consumers is that Sauternes etc. are so economically unviable that producers are giving up on making as much, and shifting to dry whites, which are easier and faster to sell, with the added plus of being cheaper to pick and produce.

Alternatively, producers might simply dispense with making a mass market sweet wine, and take the Yquem (and now Rieussec) approach of ‘resetting’ prices, under the logic that their output is not for everyone, only for those who will pay for the best.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
I’ve been hearing rumblings of this for many years…is there something new/different in 2021?

The gist of what she explains is that CB prices are stuck where they were 15 to 20 years ago, while banks who historically provided loans on the back of EP orders are less inclined to do so, resulting in an unavoidable financial problem - because even with the prices they get, those owners who sell EP do not receive the money until a year after EP. The result is an increasing number of properties changing hands - the latest being Belle Vue/Bolaire/Gironville, which has been bought by the Australian company which bought Cambon.

Some CBs have disappeared altogether after being purchased either by other CBs or increasingly by CCs. This is exacerbated by the law allowing CCs to include CB vineyards in their CCs - because the 1855 classification did not classify the land but the brands. So for example two CBs have been incorporated into Cantenac-Brown.

If the trend continues, which it probably will, there will simply be less choice for consumers and less quality wine at affordable prices.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
I’ve been hearing rumblings of this for many years…is there something new/different in 2021?

.

Some CBs have disappeared altogether after being purchased either by other CBs or increasingly by CCs. This is exacerbated by the law allowing CCs to include CB vineyards in their CCs - because the 1855 classification did not classify the land but the brands. So for example two CBs have been incorporated into Cantenac-Brown.

True, but most CB vineyards bought by GCC estates are used to make more of their second wines, not the GCC. Still it’s a win for the acquiring property as their second wines usually sell for more money than the CB.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
Interesting, thanks for the color Julian and Jeff. I am half way through Stephen Brooke’s book on Bordeaux currently (his 2017 3rd edition) and it is an enduring, repeating theme how vineyard parcels keep changing hands, finding better and higher use each time.

One other aspect, which I don’t know if Jane Anson touches on, is how French death duties - owed on land held by natural persons - seem to impose a high burden on the small estates. The larger classified growths, perhaps held in some kind of perpetual format like a corporation or insurance co., do not wear that same friction cost every 75 years or so.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
I’ve been hearing rumblings of this for many years…is there something new/different in 2021?

.

Some CBs have disappeared altogether after being purchased either by other CBs or increasingly by CCs. This is exacerbated by the law allowing CCs to include CB vineyards in their CCs - because the 1855 classification did not classify the land but the brands. So for example two CBs have been incorporated into Cantenac-Brown.

True, but most CB vineyards bought by GCC estates are used to make more of their second wines, not the GCC. Still it’s a win for the acquiring property as their second wines usually sell for more money than the CB.

Absolutely - they cherry-pick the vineyards, including the best plots in the top wines, leaving the rest for the second labels. Since the latter are double the price of most CBs, it is a double swizz for consumers. It’s about time someone did another blind tasting of CBs vs CC second wines - but I suspect I know who would win!

One other aspect, which I don’t know if Jane Anson touches on, is how French death duties - owed on land held by natural persons - seem to impose a high burden on the small estates. The larger classified growths, perhaps held in some kind of perpetual format like a corporation or insurance co., do not wear that same friction cost every 75 years or so.

No she does not mention this, which has been a problem for years, due to the increase of the price of land. For example, the average price of a hectare in Pauillac has risen by about 1200% since 1991! Little wonder that there are so few CBs left in Pauillac, nor that companies are replacing families. Whether or not one likes the idea of inherited wealth is a moot point, but it is a shame to see nobody living in the chateaux and for that reason alone, it was sad to see Lafon-R sold for example.
